1: 'Ephraim feeds on the wind and pursues the east wind all day long; they multiply falsehood and violence; they make a treaty with Assyria, and oil is carried to Egypt.' The 'east wind' (qadiym) is the sirocco — the hot, dry, desert wind that destroys crops and is uniformly associated with disaster in the OT (cf. Ezekiel 19:12, Jonah 4:8). Feeding on wind is doubly futile: neither the east wind nor the foreign alliances provide sustenance. The historical situation: Hoshea, Israel's last king, was playing both sides — paying tribute to Assyria (2 Kings 17:3) while secretly sending messengers to So of Egypt (2 Kings 17:4). 'Oil' carried to Egypt was likely an export payment for the promised military alliance.
2: 'The LORD has an indictment against Judah and will punish Jacob according to his ways; he will repay him according to his deeds.' The covenant lawsuit (riv) is the legal framework for prophecy: YHWH as prosecuting attorney, Israel/Jacob as defendant, the covenant as the law code. The shift to 'Jacob' (rather than Ephraim or Israel) is deliberate: vv3-6 will use the Jacob narrative as a mirror for the people. 'Jacob' names both the patriarch and the nation; the ancestor's story becomes the nation's story.
3: 'In the womb he took his brother by the heel, and in his manhood he strove with God.' The Jacob narrative from Genesis is invoked as a typological mirror. The heel-grasping birth (Genesis 25:26) — Jacob (Ya'aqov) means 'heel-grabber/supplanter' — represents aggressive, cunning self-advancement. 'In his manhood he strove with God' refers to the Peniel wrestling (Genesis 32:24-32), where Jacob wrestled the divine figure through the night and would not let go until he received the blessing. The contrast with Israel's current behavior is implicit: Jacob at least wrestled with God and sought his blessing — Israel has stopped engaging God entirely.
4: 'He strove with the angel and prevailed; he wept and sought his favor. He met God at Bethel, and there God spoke with him.' The Peniel wrestling (Genesis 32:24-32) is fused with the Bethel encounter (Genesis 28:10-22 and Genesis 35:9-15). Jacob 'wept and sought his favor' (yivk vayitchan-lo) — weeping in prayer, earnest supplication. This is the model being offered to Israel: genuine wrestling with God, seeking his face, not abandoning the relationship in crisis. The Bethel encounter — where God spoke to Jacob — is held up as a positive model, in contrast to Hosea's consistent condemnation of what Bethel has become (Hosea 4:15, Hosea 5:8, Hosea 10:5).
5: 'The LORD, the God of hosts, the LORD is his memorial name.' The full divine name — YHWH Elohei Tzevaot, YHWH of hosts — is the object of Jacob's wrestling, the name he sought at Peniel (Genesis 32:29: 'Why do you ask my name?'). The 'memorial name' (zikro) refers to the name by which YHWH is to be remembered across generations (cf. Exodus 3:15). Invoking the full covenant name here establishes the identity of the God with whom Jacob wrestled and the God with whom Israel is in covenant — the same YHWH whose memorial name demands ongoing acknowledgment.
6: 'So you, by the help of your God, return, hold fast to love and justice, and wait continually for your God.' The pastoral application of the Jacob typology: what Jacob did at Peniel (returned, wrestled, held on) is what Israel must do — return (tashuv) to YHWH, hold fast to chesed (covenant love) and mishpat (justice), and wait (qaveh) for God. The three imperatives capture the essence of covenant faithfulness: relational return, ethical practice, and patient hope. This is Hosea's positive program in its most concise form.
7: 'A merchant, in whose hands are false balances, he loves to oppress.' 'Canaan' (Kena'an) in the original text is both the name for Canaan/Phoenicia (the land of merchants) and the Hebrew word for 'merchant/trader' — a deliberate double meaning. Israel has become Canaanite — not just in its worship but in its commercial ethics. 'False balances' (moznei mirmah) are prohibited explicitly in Leviticus 19:35-36, Deuteronomy 25:13-16, Amos 8:5, Micah 6:11, and Proverbs 11:1. The merchant who loves (ahav) to oppress has turned covenant love toward commercial exploitation.
8: 'Ephraim has said, Ah, but I am rich; I have found wealth for myself; in all my labors, they cannot find in me iniquity or sin.' The self-justification of prosperity theology: wealth as divine approval, commercial success as moral vindication. The phrase 'I have found wealth for myself' (matsati ohn li) — ON means both 'wealth' and 'strength/vigor' — echoes the language of Deuteronomy 8:17: 'you may say in your heart, My power and the might of my hand have gotten me this wealth.' The Deuteronomic warning against prosperity-amnesia is exactly what Ephraim is doing.
9: 'I am the LORD your God from the land of Egypt; I will again make you dwell in tents, as in the days of the appointed feast.' The self-identification formula ('I am the LORD your God from the land of Egypt') echoes the covenant prologue of Exodus 20:2 and Deuteronomy 5:6 — the basis for the Ten Commandments. Making Israel 'dwell in tents' again is the wilderness-return theme: before the settlement and the prosperity that led to apostasy, Israel lived in tents before YHWH. The 'days of the appointed feast' (yemei mo'ed) specifically the Feast of Tabernacles, when Israel dwelt in booths to remember the wilderness — a yearly liturgical re-enactment that the people now must live literally.
10: 'I spoke to the prophets; it was I who multiplied visions, and through the prophets I gave parables.' The self-description of the prophetic mode: YHWH spoke through chazot (visions) and damiyti (similitudes/parables). The multiplied prophets and visions were YHWH's persistent attempt to communicate with Israel before judgment — 'I have sent to you all my servants the prophets, sending them persistently' (Jeremiah 7:25). Hosea himself is one of these multiplied prophets; the use of marriage-metaphor, agricultural imagery, and family narrative in his own book illustrates the 'parables' by which YHWH spoke.
11: 'If there is iniquity in Gilead — surely they shall come to nothing! In Gilgal they sacrifice bulls; their altars also are like stone heaps on the furrows of the field.' Gilead (the Transjordanian territory) and Gilgal (the Jordan valley sanctuary, first campsite after the crossing) are both condemned as centers of false worship. The 'stone heaps on furrows' (galim al-talme sadai) is a precise agricultural image: fieldstones cleared from plowed ground and piled at the edges — the altars are as numerous and as worthless as these field-clearing heaps. The false altars have proliferated like the debris of agricultural labor.
12: 'Jacob fled to the land of Aram; there Israel served for a wife, and for a wife he kept sheep.' The third Jacob narrative reference: the flight to Haran (Genesis 28:10, Hosea 29:1-30), the 14 years of service for Leah and Rachel. The mention of Jacob 'serving' (abad) for a wife is pointed: the patriarch who strove with God and wrestled a blessing submitted to human labor for love. Israel who will not serve YHWH is contrasted with Jacob who willingly served Laban. The shepherd-service of Jacob prefigures the prophet-service that will preserve Israel (v13).
13: 'By a prophet the LORD brought Israel up from Egypt, and by a prophet he was kept.' Moses as the prophet who brought Israel out of Egypt (Exod 3-14) and 'kept' (nishmar) Israel in the wilderness — the verb suggests careful preservation, like a shepherd guarding a flock. The Jacob-who-served-for-a-wife (v12) contrast with Moses-who-served-for-Israel sets up a typology: the covenant mediator who gave himself for the people preserved by divine care. The prophet's role in Israel's history is foundational — Moses at the Exodus, the prophets in the monarchy. Hosea himself stands in this line.
14: 'Ephraim has given bitter provocation; so his Lord will leave his bloodguilt on him and will repay him for his disgraceful deeds.' The 'bitter provocation' (hikis Ephraim tamrurim) is the culmination of the Jacob typology: Jacob strove with God and prevailed; Ephraim provokes God and faces judgment. 'Bloodguilt' (damav) — the unpurged guilt of bloodshed (cf. the blood of Jezreel, Hosea 1:4) left on Ephraim without expiation. The 'disgraceful deeds' (cherpato, reproaches) that YHWH will return: the covenant mechanism of measure-for-measure judgment, the wages of rebellion paid back in kind.
